message.content: Below are **commonly missed SEO / structured‑data test categories** for modern web apps, with: - the **check** you should run - **what it verifies / why it matters** - an **exact authoritative URL** (Google Search Central, web.dev, Schema.org, etc.) I’ve focused only on categories that are often *omitted* from generic SEO checklists and mapped them to official guidance. --- ### 1. Pagination handling (rel=next/prev, view‑all, infinite scroll) #### 1.1 Legacy `rel="next"` / `rel="prev"` deprecation - **Check** - Confirm you are **not relying on `rel="next"` / `rel="prev"`** link tags for pagination logic. - Ensure paginated series are discoverable via internal links and standard crawling. - **What it verifies** - Google **no longer uses** `rel="next"` / `rel="prev"` for indexing; you must not depend on it to consolidate signals or for discovery. - **Authoritative URL** - Google Search Central: *“Pagination”* (note and tweets on deprecation are referenced from this help ecosystem) - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/crawl-budget` (section: “Faceted navigation and session identifiers” – discusses pagination and crawl budget) - `https://developers.google.com/search/blog/2019/03/pagination-with-relnext-and-relprev` (official blog announcement of deprecation) #### 1.2 Infinite scroll + History API + paginated URLs - **Check** - For infinite scroll / “load more” UIs, verify there are **crawlable paginated URLs** (e.g., `?page=2`) accessible via links, *in addition* to JS behaviors. - Ensure **History API** or URL fragments do not hide content from bots. - **What it verifies** - Search engines can **reach all items** even if JS fails; Google requires that infinite scroll be paired with **paginated, link‑accessible pages**. - **Authoritative URL** - Google Search Central (old but still cited pattern): - `https://developers.google.com/search/blog/2014/02/infinite-scroll-search-friendly` - General guidance on JS navigation and crawlability: - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/javascript/javascript-seo-basics` #### 1.3 “View all” pages - **Check** - If you provide a **“view all”** version that lists all items, verify it: - is not excessively heavy to load - is crawlable and indexable (unless deliberately blocked) - is consistent with paginated versions and canonical setup. - **What it verifies** - A single, comprehensive page can act as a **canonical target** when practical; avoids thin or fragmented indexing of long series. - **Authoritative URL** - Google Search Central (pagination / view‑all discussion in legacy guidance): - `https://developers.google.com/search/blog/2011/09/pagination-with-relnext-and-relprev` (still referenced for view‑all concepts) --- ### 2. JavaScript SEO – rendering, hydration, and content parity #### 2.1 JS‑dependent content rendering - **Check** - Use **URL Inspection** in Search Console and “View crawled page” to compare **Rendered HTML vs. source HTML**. - Confirm that **critical content, links, and meta tags** appear in the rendered HTML that Googlebot sees. - **What it verifies** - Ensures Google’s **two‑wave indexing** (HTML crawl then JS render) can see your primary content; avoids empty/partial pages. - **Authoritative URLs** - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/javascript/javascript-seo-basics` - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/javascript/javascript-seo-faq` #### 2.2 Hydration issues / client‑side rewrites - **Check** - Confirm your framework (React, Vue, Next, etc.) does **not remove or significantly change** content after initial render in ways that differ from what Googlebot indexes. - Check for **content flicker or replacement** (e.g., placeholders, skeleton screens) where the DOM Google indexes is missing real text. - **What it verifies** - Avoids **content mismatch** between server-rendered / first render and hydrated client app, which can lead to partial or misleading indexing. - **Authoritative URL** - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/javascript/javascript-seo-basics` (sections on dynamic rendering and content parity) - `https://web.dev/rendering-on-the-web/` (web.dev – explains hydration and SEO impact) #### 2.3 JS‑controlled meta tags and canonical - **Check** - Ensure critical tags (``, `<meta name="description">`, `<link rel="canonical">`, `<meta name="robots">`) are **present in initial HTML** or **stable** after JS execution. - **What it verifies** - Prevents Google from indexing pages with **default or missing metadata** due to late JS mutations that may not be used. - **Authoritative URL** - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/javascript/javascript-seo-basics` (meta tags and JS section) --- ### 3. Canonical + `noindex` and other directive conflicts #### 3.1 Canonical pointing to indexed URL while page is `noindex` - **Check** - Detect URLs with **`<meta name="robots" content="noindex">`** or **X‑Robots‑Tag: noindex** that also specify a **canonical link to a different URL**. - **What it verifies** - Google may treat a **`noindex` page as a weak canonical candidate**, or ignore the canonical; you should avoid ambiguous signals. - **Authoritative URL** - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/consolidate-duplicate-urls` (canonicalization best practices) - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/robots-meta-tag` (robots meta interactions) #### 3.2 Multiple conflicting canonicals - **Check** - Validate that each page has **at most one `rel="canonical"`** tag in HTML and that HTTP headers do not define an additional conflicting canonical. - **What it verifies** - Conflicting canonicals reduce Google’s confidence in your preferred URL and can lead to **unexpected canonical selection**. - **Authoritative URL** - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/consolidate-duplicate-urls` (section: “Use one canonical URL per page”) --- ### 4. Structured data vs. visible content mismatch - **Check** - For each structured data type (Product, Article, FAQ, Review, etc.), verify that: - **Key properties** (e.g., name, price, rating, FAQ questions/answers) are **present on the page in visible content**. - Markup does not include **fake or misleading data** (e.g., reviews not visible to users). - **What it verifies** - Avoids **structured data spam** / mismatches that can trigger manual actions or loss of rich results. - **Authoritative URLs** - General SD guidelines (visibility, no misleading content): - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/structured-data/guidelines` - Spam policies including structured data: - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/essentials/spam-policies` --- ### 5. AMP validity (for sites still using AMP) - **Check** - Validate AMP pages with the **AMP Test** or `#development=1` console log; ensure **no AMP validation errors** and that required structured data is correct. - Confirm canonical relationships: - AMP page has `<link rel="canonical" href="non-amp-url">`. - Non‑AMP has `<link rel="amphtml" href="amp-url">` if using that pattern. - **What it verifies** - Only **valid AMP pages** are eligible for AMP‑specific features in Search. - **Authoritative URLs** - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/amp` - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/amp-test` --- ### 6. Favicon and Web App Manifest #### 6.1 Favicon for Search - **Check** - Ensure a **favicon** is accessible and declared according to Google’s favicon guidelines: - Correct file type and size (min 48×48, square). - Properly referenced in HTML `<head>`. - **What it verifies** - Favicon can appear next to your site name in mobile (and some desktop) search results. - **Authoritative URL** - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/favicon-in-search` #### 6.2 Web App Manifest (PWA signals) - **Check** - Verify that `link rel="manifest"` references a valid **Web App Manifest** with correct **`name`, `short_name`, `icons`, `start_url`, `display`** fields. - **What it verifies** - While not a direct ranking factor, a correct manifest underpins **installability and PWA UX**, often overlooked in SEO tech audits. - **Authoritative URL** - `https://web.dev/add-manifest/` - `https://w3c.github.io/manifest/` (Web App Manifest spec; authoritative but not Google-specific) --- ### 7. Breadcrumb structured data vs. visual breadcrumbs - **Check** - Ensure **visual breadcrumbs** on the page match **`BreadcrumbList` structured data**: - Same hierarchy and labels. - URLs in `item` match actual breadcrumb links. - **What it verifies** - Avoids **inconsistent breadcrumb trails** between visible content and rich results, which can reduce trust and eligibility. - **Authoritative URLs** - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/structured-data/breadcrumb` - Schema.org type: - `https://schema.org/BreadcrumbList` --- ### 8. Redirect‑based cloaking and user‑agent‑specific behavior - **Check** - Confirm there are **no redirects that only apply to certain user agents** (e.g., redirecting users to a different content experience while serving Googlebot the original page). - Use log analysis and tools that simulate **Googlebot vs. standard browser** to compare responses. - **What it verifies** - Prevents **cloaking**, including redirect‑based cloaking, which violates Google’s spam policies and can incur manual actions. - **Authoritative URLs** - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/essentials/spam-policies` (see “Cloaking” and “Sneaky redirects”) --- ### 9. Mixed content on HTTPS sites - **Check** - On HTTPS pages, scan for **HTTP resources** (images, scripts, iframes, CSS). - Ensure all subresources are loaded via **HTTPS** or upgraded URLs. - **What it verifies** - Mixed content can lead to **blocked resources** (especially scripts/styles) which affect rendering, Core Web Vitals, and user trust. - **Authoritative URLs** - `https://web.dev/what-is-mixed-content/` - `https://web.dev/fixing-mixed-content/` --- ### 10. Lazy‑loaded content and image crawlability - **Check** - Verify lazy‑loaded content and images: - Do not require **user gestures** (e.g., scroll past a threshold that Googlebot might not trigger). - Use patterns that work with Google’s JS rendering (e.g., `IntersectionObserver`). - Include proper `<img>` elements or `<picture>` rather than CSS background images when SEO is needed. - **What it verifies** - Ensures Google can **discover and index lazy‑loaded content and images** that matter for search and image search. - **Authoritative URLs** - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/lazy-loading` - `https://web.dev/lazy-loading/` --- ### 11. Meta robots vs. X‑Robots‑Tag conflicts - **Check** - Compare **HTML `<meta name="robots">`** with **HTTP X‑Robots‑Tag** headers for the same URL. - Identify any conflicts (e.g., header `noindex` but meta `index,follow`). - **What it verifies** - Google follows **a precedence order**; conflicting directives can cause unexpected deindexing or crawling behavior. - **Authoritative URLs** - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/robots-meta-tag` - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/robots-meta-tag#xrobotstag` --- ### 12. Trailing‑slash and redirect loop / chain problems - **Check** - Ensure **consistent canonicalization** of trailing vs. non‑trailing slash URLs: - One version is used internally and as canonical. - No **redirect loop** (e.g., `/page` → `/page/` → `/page`). - Avoid long redirect chains (multiple hops). - **What it verifies** - Prevents **crawl budget waste, soft 404 patterns, and canonical confusion** from loops/chains. - **Authoritative URLs** - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/consolidate-duplicate-urls` (canonicalization and consistent URL patterns) - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/404-pages` (soft 404 considerations) --- ### 13. Internationalized domain and URL handling - **Check** - For **IDNs / non‑ASCII URLs**: - Confirm correct use of **punycode** at DNS and HTTP level. - Ensure hreflang and canonical tags use **fully qualified, correctly encoded URLs**. - For multi‑regional / multilingual setups: - Verify correct **`hreflang`** implementation and that each language/region URL is **crawlable** and **self‑referential with hreflang**. - **What it verifies** - Avoids **indexing anomalies** for IDN domains and prevents incorrect **language/region targeting**. - **Authoritative URLs** - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/localized-versions` (hreflang and different URLs) - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/getting-started` - `https://support.google.com/webmasters/answer/182192` (international domains and URLs) --- ### 14. Meta robots + canonical + hreflang interaction - **Check** - Ensure pages that participate in **hreflang clusters** are **indexable** (no `noindex` in meta or X‑Robots) and have a **self‑canonical** or canonical within the cluster. - **What it verifies** - Hreflang is ignored if the target page is **noindexed** or non‑canonical in a way that conflicts with the cluster; frequently missed in audits. - **Authoritative URLs** - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/localized-versions#technical-guidelines` --- ### 15. Robots.txt vs. meta robots for JS/CSS and API endpoints - **Check** - Confirm **critical JS/CSS files** and **API endpoints** used to render content are **not blocked** by `robots.txt`. - **What it verifies** - Blocking these can prevent Google from rendering the page correctly, leading to **misinterpreted layout or missing content**. - **Authoritative URLs** - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/robots/intro` - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/javascript/javascript-seo-basics` (section on blocking resources) --- If you want, I can turn this into a **machine‑readable QA checklist** (e.g., CSV or JSON with “test name / check / signals / authoritative URL”) that you can feed into automated auditing tools.
